sources.verification_method: Founded 2020. CONFIRMED via web_fetch of posthog.com/about 2026-05-23 (primary company source): 'PostHog was hatched in Y Combinator's W20 batch. We launched on Hacker News with our MVP in 2020 – just 4 weeks after we started writing code.' Co-founders James Hawkins and Tim Glaser (both Co-CEO). Cross-confirmed YC profile + Crunchbase + PrivCo. Per METHODOLOGY 7.1, founded_year=2020 (legal entity / company origin coincide with project origin here — no entity-vs-code gap as in Sentry). CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

PrivCo company profile lists PostHog, Inc. HQ at 2261 Market Street Suite 4008, San Francisco, California 94114, US. ISO 3166-1 alpha-2: US. NOTE: PostHog is a remote-first company with no traditional central office; the SF address is the legal/operational anchor. Cross-confirmed with Exa websets directory (San Francisco). Source.type='other' (PrivCo is private-market data provider not in schema enum, analog to Crunchbase/Tracxn handling). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM — secondary aggregators; PENDING a primary-source HQ confirmation (posthog.com/about or SEC/Delaware filing).
PrivCo specifies San Francisco, CA. Atlas canonical short form per METHODOLOGY 7.2: 'San Francisco'. Same MEDIUM/remote-first caveat as hq_country.

Official PostHog blog post titled '"E"xciting news' announcing the USD 75M Series E led by Peak XV Partners at a USD 1.4B valuation (unicorn status), late September 2025. Per the schema enum, Series E maps to 'series-e-plus'. Primary source (company blog) per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1) — direct official statement. Cross-confirmed: Peak XV Partners X/Twitter announcement (2025-09-30), TheSaaSNews, YourStory. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

Tracxn 2026 PostHog funding profile + summary reporting of USD 194M total raised across 7 rounds since 2020 founding. Manual sanity-check: pre-Series-E total reported ~USD 119M; + USD 75M Series E = USD 194M (consistent). Per METHODOLOGY 7.4 private-company convention. Source.type='other' (Tracxn private-market provider). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM — secondary convergence; per the officially-confirmed-vs-market-reported funding rule (Grafana precedent), the most recent ROUND (Series E) has a primary source, but the AGGREGATE total relies on secondary convergence.

Wellfound (AngelList) PostHog funding page independently lists the round history converging on the ~USD 194M aggregate. Independent corroboration per METHODOLOGY 3.2 (≥2 sources for high-stakes claim). Source.type='other'.

Official PostHog Series E blog declares the USD 1.4B post-money valuation. Primary source. Cross-confirmed: TheSaaSNews ('PostHog Raises $75M Series E at $1.4B Valuation'), Peak XV Partners announcement, CompWorth, BuiltIn SF. Multiple independent sources converge on USD 1.4B. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.
TheSaaSNews independently corroborates the USD 1.4B Series E valuation. Cross-confirmation per METHODOLOGY 3.2.
Crunchbase PostHog organization profile lists status as private, VC-backed (latest round Series E, Sept 2025). No SEC filings, no ticker. is_public=false. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

PostHog/posthog primary repo root LICENSE is the MIT (Expat) License, EXCEPT content under the 'ee/' directory which carries a separate proprietary license (see ee/LICENSE source below). Per Atlas convention (Grafana precedent: primary_current = license of the core primary_repo), primary_current='MIT'. CONFIRMED via web_fetch of the github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/master/LICENSE blob at master HEAD on 2026-05-23 (method: Claude web_fetch of the live GitHub blob page, equivalent fidelity to a browser read of the file at HEAD). Exact file text: 'Copyright (c) 2020-2025 PostHog Inc. Portions of this software are licensed as follows: * All content that resides under the "ee/" directory ... is licensed under the license defined in "ee/LICENSE". * ... Content outside of the above ... is available under the "MIT Expat" license as defined below.' followed by the standard MIT Expat permission text. 25 lines, 1.53 KB. SPDX-canonical short form = 'MIT'. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

The 'ee/' (enterprise edition) directory carries a separate proprietary license. CRITICAL ATLAS OBSERVATION: this is open-core via directory segregation (permissive MIT core + proprietary ee/ subdirectory), NOT a relicensing of the core. PostHog's core OSS license appears to have NEVER changed from MIT since 2020 founding → primary_first='MIT' = primary_current='MIT', license_history=[]. This would make PostHog the FIRST Atlas record where the core OSS license never changed (MongoDB Apache→SSPL, CockroachDB Apache→BSL→CSL, Sentry BSD→FSL, Grafana Apache→AGPL all relicensed their cores). PostHog additionally maintains a 'posthog-foss' repository purged of all proprietary code (100% FOSS). ee/LICENSE CONFIRMED via web_fetch at master HEAD 2026-05-23: canonical name 'The PostHog Enterprise license (the "Enterprise License")', Copyright (c) 2020-2025 PostHog Inc. — a proprietary source-available license: production use requires agreement to the PostHog Subscription Terms (posthog.com/terms) and a valid Enterprise license for the correct number of user seats; modification/testing permitted without subscription; copy/merge/publish/distribute/sell forbidden. 36 lines, 2.13 KB. The 2020-2025 copyright span on the root LICENSE is consistent with the core having been MIT since founding. STILL PENDING (only remaining license TODO): Wayback-check the root LICENSE across 2020/2022/2024 to definitively confirm no quiet core relicense ever occurred (validates license_history=[] to HIGH). CONFIDENCE: HIGH for current MIT-core + ee/ proprietary structure; MEDIUM-HIGH for license_history=[] pending the Wayback historical confirmation.

dual_license=false per METHODOLOGY 7.9 strict definition (same source code offered under two concurrent user-selectable licenses). PostHog is NOT dual-license: the MIT core and the proprietary ee/ are DIFFERENT code in DIFFERENT directories — directory-segregated open-core, not the same code under two licenses. It is also NOT 'tiered source-available' (BSL/FSL/SSPL/CSL): the core is genuinely OSI-approved MIT, not a source-available license. Analogous in resolution to Grafana's core-vs-plugins and Sentry's server-vs-SDK license heterogeneity (documented, but dual_license=false).

cla_required=false. UPGRADED LOW→MEDIUM-HIGH via web_fetch of posthog.com/docs/contribute 2026-05-23. The contribute page documents the full contribution flow ('Anyone can contribute code... make your change and submit a pull request to the posthog repository') and its 'Licensing' section ('Most of PostHog's code is under the MIT license... Code of paid features is covered by a proprietary license') WITHOUT any Contributor License Agreement step — no CLA mentioned anywhere in the contribution path. Per METHODOLOGY 7.10 hierarchy: the canonical out-of-band contribution page describes contribution with no CLA. RESIDUAL UNCERTAINTY (minor): a definitive negative would also confirm the absence of a cla-assistant bot on a recent PostHog/posthog PR — left as an optional final check. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM-HIGH. Distinct from MongoDB/CockroachDB/Grafana (cla_required=true); matches Sentry (false).

type='saas-with-oss-marketing' per METHODOLOGY disambiguation rule (the load-bearing test: can a serious user run this in production and receive a first-class experience?). The evidence points consistently to NO: (a) self-host Kubernetes/scalable deployment deprecated and no longer supported; (b) only a 'hobby' Docker Compose build remains; (c) self-host explicitly 'not recommended' for scale; (d) new features ship Cloud-first; (e) commercial support is not centered on self-host; (f) product narrative oriented to PostHog Cloud. This moves the business center of gravity to SaaS even though the core code remains MIT. CRITICAL: this classification is NOT because the OSS is fake — the opposite. PostHog's MIT core is probably the most genuinely-open OSS in the Atlas so far. The classification is about operational reality, not license posture. It is NOT 'hosted-first' (Supabase / Cal.com / Plausible have genuinely production-grade self-host; PostHog deprecated its production self-host). CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the classification; the underlying self-host facts are PENDING Pablo browser-validation of posthog.com/docs/self-host + disclaimer (upgrades the supporting evidence MEDIUM→HIGH).

primary_revenue_stream='cloud'. PostHog Cloud is the recommended and operationally-encouraged option; all paid features and new features are Cloud-only; self-hosted is unsupported and not monetized as a material enterprise self-host product (contrast Grafana/MongoDB which sell self-hosted Enterprise licenses). Enterprise self-hosted is not a material revenue stream. CONFIDENCE: HIGH directionally; PostHog has not published a revenue-stream split (no public breakdown), but the Cloud-first posture is unambiguous from product + pricing + self-host docs.

pricing.free_tier_notes: PostHog Cloud has a generous monthly-resetting free tier with no credit card required; the company states 'more than 90% of companies use PostHog for free.' CONFIRMED via web_fetch of posthog.com/pricing 2026-05-23. Free monthly allotments per product (reset monthly, retained even after upgrading to pay-as-you-go): 1M product analytics events, 5K session recordings (web) + 2.5K mobile recordings, 1M feature flag requests, 100K error-tracking exceptions, 1,500 survey responses, 1M data-warehouse rows (+ unlimited free historical syncs), 10K data-pipeline trigger events + 1M batch-export rows, 100K LLM-analytics events, 2K PostHog AI credits (~USD 20), 10K workflow messages per channel, 50GB ingested logs. Free plan: 1 project, 1-year data retention, unlimited team members, community support. Separately, the MIT-licensed core can be self-hosted free indefinitely (hobby/unsupported — see self_hosted_restrictions). CONFIDENCE: HIGH (live pricing page).
pricing.self_hosted_restrictions: The MIT-licensed PostHog core can be self-hosted free indefinitely, BUT PostHog has operationally deprecated production self-hosting. Per posthog.com/docs/self-host + the open-source self-host disclaimer (browser-validation recommended): (a) new Kubernetes/scalable deployments are NO LONGER SUPPORTED; only a 'hobby' Docker Compose build remains; (b) self-hosting is explicitly 'not recommended' — requires significant infra expertise, manual updates, does not scale; (c) NO commercial support for self-hosted; (d) paid/enterprise features (ee/ directory) require a PostHog license and are not in the free self-host; (e) new features ship Cloud-only and self-hosted deployments miss continuous improvements. This is the operational core of the PostHog record: the OSS is genuinely MIT and free, but PostHog has migrated to a Cloud-first commercial posture — distinct from Grafana (whose AGPL OSS IS production-grade self-hostable at any scale, 'Mimir scales to 1B series').

sources.verification_method: pricing_model='usage' per METHODOLOGY 7.11. PostHog's canonical self-serve bill is pure metered consumption per product (product analytics per-event with volume step-downs USD 0.000050→0.000009/event; session replay per-recording USD 0.005→0.0015; plus per-unit feature flag requests, survey responses, error exceptions, logs, AI credits). There is NO per-seat fee ('no per-seat tax') and NO fixed platform base fee on pay-as-you-go. Per 7.11, 'pure usage applies when pricing is dominantly metered consumption with no fixed-tier structure (e.g., AWS Lambda per request)'. The multiple product axes are all the SAME dimension TYPE (metered consumption); the 7.11 'hybrid' examples instead MIX dimension types (capacity + usage, e.g. MongoDB cluster + storage + egress). PostHog has no capacity/seat/time-minimum dimension in its canonical bill → 'usage', NOT 'hybrid'. The Enterprise plan adds a time-based subscription base fee (reported ~USD 2,000/mo) but that is a non-canonical enterprise tier, documented in metadata.notes. CONFIDENCE: HIGH on the usage classification; MEDIUM on exact per-unit rates pending browser-validation.

cloud_entry_price_usd_month=null per METHODOLOGY 7.17. Free tier is excluded (captured via free_tier_exists). PostHog's only paid self-serve option below Enterprise is pure pay-as-you-go usage with NO fixed monthly floor ($0 base + per-unit). Per 7.17, variable/pay-per-use tiers without a fixed monthly floor are excluded unless they are the only paid option — here they ARE the only self-serve paid option, but there is no fixed price POINT to capture (the floor is $0, which is the free tier). Setting null is the honest read: PostHog has no fixed-price paid cloud tier. CROSS-RECORD OBSERVATION: this is the FIRST Atlas record with cloud_entry_price=null — distinct from Grafana USD 19, Sentry USD 26, MongoDB USD 58.40, CockroachDB USD 438, all of which had a lowest fixed-price paid tier. CANDIDATE METHODOLOGY 7.17 CLARIFICATION (post-Free-Preview): the 'uncapped, unfloored pure-usage as only paid self-serve option' case resolves to null, not 0 (0 would conflate with the free tier). CONFIRMED via web_fetch of posthog.com/pricing 2026-05-23: the Pay-as-you-go plan is explicitly 'Starts at $0/mo' with 'Base price: $0'; pricing FAQ confirms 'Are there any minimums or annual commitments? Nope.' No fixed-price paid cloud tier exists below the Enterprise add-on. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

enterprise_min_declared_usd=2000. CONFIRMED via web_fetch of posthog.com/pricing 2026-05-23: the pricing-page calculator lists three 'Platform packages' add-ons — Boost USD 250/mo, Scale USD 750/mo, Enterprise USD 2,000/mo. The Enterprise add-on (USD 2,000/mo) is the published Enterprise entry; it layers on top of usage and adds dedicated support, RBAC, SSO enforcement, and (per the plan comparison) compliance/governance features. PERIOD NOTE: this figure is MONTHLY (USD 2,000/mo ≈ USD 24,000/yr annualized), distinct from Grafana's enterprise_min_declared_usd=25,000 which was an ANNUAL spend-commit floor. Annualized, PostHog's Enterprise add-on (~USD 24K/yr) sits notably close to Grafana's published USD 25K/yr floor — a cross-record observation. CANDIDATE METHODOLOGY CLARIFICATION (alongside the 7.17 work): enterprise_min_declared_usd needs an explicit period convention (monthly vs annual) to be cross-record comparable; PostHog (monthly add-on) vs Grafana (annual commit) currently store incomparable raw figures. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the published USD 2,000/mo figure; the per-tier feature contents are MEDIUM pending Phase 5 feature_gating cross-check.

PostHog pricing page advertises a transparent, usage-based model with a generous free tier and no credit card required. free_tier_exists=true. Cross-confirmed across 2026 pricing aggregators (userorbit, checkthat.ai, usercall, costbench). PENDING Pablo browser-validation of the live page + Wayback save.

PostHog self-host docs confirm the MIT core is self-hostable free indefinitely (hobby build), while explicitly stating self-hosting is not recommended, unsupported, non-scalable, and missing paid + new features. self_hosted_free_indefinite=true (the OSS IS free indefinitely), with the heavy operational restrictions captured in self_hosted_restrictions. Cross-confirmed with posthog.com/docs/self-host/open-source/disclaimer.

feature_gating.notes: HEADLINE PHASE 5 FINDING (predicted by Pablo): advanced_analytics='oss' BREAKS the 'partial' pattern shared by MongoDB/CockroachDB/Sentry/Grafana (all 'partial'). For PostHog the advanced analytics IS the open core — product analytics, web analytics, funnels/paths/retention/correlation, SQL access (HogQL), session replay, feature flags, experiments, surveys are all in the MIT-licensed product and on the generous free Cloud tier. This is a category-driven divergence: an analytics-bi company's core value (analytics depth) sits in the OSS/free layer, whereas for databases/observability the advanced analytics/reporting layer was the gated upsell. Minor paid exception: Group Analytics is a per-event add-on; this does not move the classification off 'oss' for the core. sso_saml='commercial' but with a generosity nuance: basic OAuth SSO (Google/GitHub/GitLab) is FREE on ALL plans incl. free tier; SAML + SSO ENFORCEMENT are gated behind a Platform package add-on. Classified 'commercial' for cross-record consistency with Grafana (which also had OAuth-in-OSS, SAML-in-Enterprise → commercial), with the OAuth-free generosity noted. rbac_fine_grained='commercial' (RBAC is an explicit Platform package add-on per the pricing page) — consistent with all 4 prior records. audit_logs='commercial' (compliance/governance, Enterprise add-on — CONFIDENCE MEDIUM, inferred from Enterprise compliance bundle, pending a direct PostHog docs cite). sla_support_tier='commercial': community support (free) → standard email/Slack support (Slack over USD 2k/mo) → dedicated support (Enterprise add-on). multi_region_ha='oss': PostHog Cloud offers US (Virginia) and EU (Frankfurt) regions on ALL tiers INCLUDING the free tier (data residency not commercially gated); self-host multi-region is the operator's choice under MIT. SEMANTIC CAVEAT: PostHog is analytics-bi, so this reading does NOT test the tentative observability multi_region_ha='oss' signal (Grafana+Sentry, N=2) — it is a standalone analytics-bi data point. Candidate methodological note: multi_region_ha may need a category-specific semantic for analytics-bi (data residency) the way the observability-vs-database split was drawn during Sentry collection. white_label='na' (PostHog is a product-analytics platform, not OEM-embeddable). api_rate_limits='na' (API access included on all plans; rate limits are service-stability, not a marketed tier feature). CONFIDENCE: HIGH for advanced_analytics, multi_region_ha, sso_saml, rbac_fine_grained, sla_support_tier (all from posthog.com/pricing web_fetch 2026-05-23); MEDIUM for audit_logs pending a direct docs cite.

sources.verification_method: posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23): 'All plans include: ... Google, Github, and Gitlab SSO' (basic OAuth SSO free on all tiers); 'Platform packages: Choose from extra features, like dedicated support, RBAC and SSO enforcement.' SAML + SSO enforcement are gated behind a Platform package add-on → sso_saml='commercial'. OAuth-free generosity noted (more open than peers). CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23): RBAC listed explicitly as a Platform package add-on ('extra features, like dedicated support, RBAC and SSO enforcement'). rbac_fine_grained='commercial'. Consistent with all 4 prior Atlas records. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

audit_logs='commercial' — inferred from the Enterprise add-on compliance/governance bundle (SOC 2 Type II, RBAC, SSO enforcement gated to Enterprise per the Platform packages). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM — not directly cited as a standalone line item on the pricing page; pending a direct PostHog docs reference for audit-log gating. Consistent with all 4 prior records (audit_logs='commercial').

posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23): support tiers to commercial relationship — Free = community support; Pay-as-you-go = standard email support (Slack-based support for accounts over USD 2k/mo); Enterprise add-on = dedicated support. sla_support_tier='commercial'. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23): the Free plan and Pay-as-you-go plan both expose a 'Cloud region: US (Virginia) / EU (Frankfurt)' selector — multi-region data residency is available on ALL tiers including free, not commercially gated → multi_region_ha='oss'. Self-host (MIT) multi-region is the operator's choice. SEMANTIC CAVEAT: analytics-bi category, so this does NOT test the observability multi_region_ha='oss' signal (Grafana+Sentry). CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the gating fact.

posthog.com/pricing + posthog.com/about (web_fetch 2026-05-23): PostHog's analytics suite (product analytics, web analytics, funnels/paths/retention, SQL/HogQL, session replay, feature flags, experiments, surveys) is in the MIT-licensed core and on the generous free Cloud tier. advanced_analytics='oss' — analytics IS the open product, not a gated upsell. This BREAKS the 'partial' pattern of the 4 prior records (category-driven: analytics-bi core value lives in OSS). Minor paid exception (Group Analytics add-on) does not move the classification. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

strategic_signals.pricing_changes_l24m: PostHog's documented L24M pricing posture is repeated price CUTS, not increases — the opposite of the typical SaaS pattern. Per posthog.com/about and posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23): 'Price cuts instead of increases', 'We aim to match the cheapest competitor for each product at every scale', 'We make a profit with every product [so] we don't have loss-leader products that will go up in pricing later', and the co-founder bio quip 'When James isn't thinking about how to cut prices (again)...'. This is itself a strategic signal: PostHog treats low/declining price as a reputation-and-distribution moat (it grows via internet reputation rather than sales). No Wayback pricing diff was captured this session (web.archive.org URLs were not in the web_fetch provenance set) — a Wayback save + historical diff of posthog.com/pricing remains a forward-audit TODO. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the qualitative price-cut posture (company-stated, multiple pages); the precise per-product L24M rate changes are not individually diffed.

sources.verification_method: Official PostHog blog 'PostHog raises a series D (and a small C)' — USD 70M Series D led by Stripe at a USD 920M valuation, ~2025-06 (BuiltIn SF / Crunchbase News date the round 2025-06-16). Per METHODOLOGY 7.16, private VC round → captured. Primary source. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for round + lead + amount; the small concurrent Series C noted in the same post is folded into total_raised, not given a separate funding_rounds_l24m entry (no separate amount/lead disclosed for it).

Official PostHog blog '"E"xciting news' — USD 75M Series E led by Peak XV Partners at a USD 1.4B valuation, announced ~2025-09-30 (Peak XV X/Twitter announcement 2025-09-30). Primary source. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

posthog.com/customers (web_fetch 2026-05-23) lists named customers with linked case studies: Y Combinator (experiments, product analytics — 'world's premier startup accelerator'), Supabase, ElevenLabs, Lovable, Arena, ResearchGate, Exa, Hasura. These 8 are the explicitly-NAMED, case-study-linked customers (HIGH confidence). The page additionally lists several customers by description only (e.g., 'open source LLMs', 'the MacOS Spotlight Apple should have built', 'they make airplanes', 'crypto wallet') — Atlas does NOT capture these because the names are not explicitly stated on the page (epistemic discipline: no guessing logos). Schema maxItems=10; 8 captured. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the 8 named.

license_changes_l24m=[]. PostHog's core has been MIT since 2020 founding and the LICENSE at master HEAD remains MIT Expat (web_fetch 2026-05-23). The ee/ proprietary directory is an open-core productization choice that predates the L24M window (cutoff 2024-05-23) and is not a relicense of the core. No license change occurred in L24M. Per Atlas convention, the (non-)event is captured here as []; the MIT-core continuity is in license.license_history=[]. (HIGH for current state; the full-history no-quiet-relicense confirmation is the pending Wayback TODO noted in metadata.notes.)
posthog.com/about + posthog.com/pricing (web_fetch 2026-05-23) document the price-cut posture quoted in the pricing_changes_l24m field. Company-stated, cross-page. CONFIDENCE: HIGH qualitative.

metadata.notes: RECORD STATUS: DRAFT IN-PROGRESS as of 2026-05-23. Phases 0-7 done (0-5 + 7 on 2026-05-23; Phase 6 github velocity on 2026-05-23 via Pablo's machine — see PHASE 6 block below). REMAINING: (i) contributors_total + contributors_l12m backfill (the only two unfilled github fields, currently null); (ii) Phase 8 verification — Wayback license-history check, Wayback pricing save, 24h cool-off, Day-2 (cool-off NOT satisfiable until >=2026-05-24). NOT a verified record; do not flip to verified until all phases complete + browser validations + 24h cool-off + Day-2 sanity. CATEGORY DECISION 2026-05-23: reclassified from the original stub 'observability' to 'analytics-bi'. PostHog's center of gravity is product analytics (product/web analytics, session replay, feature flags, experimentation, surveys, data warehouse, CDP), with only a minority observability toehold (error tracking, LLM observability). Grafana and Sentry are observability infrastructure; PostHog measures product/users, not systems. METHODOLOGICAL CONSEQUENCE: PostHog as 'analytics-bi' opens a new category (N=1) and does NOT test the tentative observability multi_region_ha='oss' signal (which stays at N=2 within observability, Grafana+Sentry). Forcing PostHog into 'observability' to extend that series would have contaminated the cross-record signal. CORE INSIGHT (the value of this record, per Pablo 2026-05-23): PostHog is the first DEPLOYMENT/COMMERCIALIZATION pivot in the dataset, NOT a licensing pivot. The other four records (MongoDB, CockroachDB, Sentry, Grafana) all pivoted their LICENSE (permissive/OSI → source-available or copyleft). PostHog kept a permissive MIT core that remains permissive, and instead migrated the COMMERCIAL PRODUCT operationally to SaaS-first (deprecated production self-hosting, Cloud-only paid + new features). license_history=[] reflects this: the core never relicensed. This is another value-capture strategy over OSS, and probably a more culturally 'modern' one: keep the core open (MIT), but make operating the serious product outside the cloud cease to be attractive. Where MongoDB/CockroachDB/Sentry/Grafana defended monetization via a LICENSING pivot, PostHog defended monetization via an OPERATIONAL/PRODUCT pivot. This distinction (licensing pivot vs commercialization pivot) should appear textually here and is candidate material for a future HYPOTHESES / analysis writeup on OSS monetization strategies. CANDIDATE METHODOLOGICAL OBSERVATION (Pablo 2026-05-23, NOT an amendment today — flag alongside the provisional 7.21): PostHog exposes a real semantic limitation of the current schema. self_hosted_free_indefinite=true captures that self-host is AVAILABLE, but not that it is OPERATIONALLY ENCOURAGED or PRODUCTION-GRADE. For PostHog, 'available' = true but 'production-grade / first-class self-host' = effectively false. A future schema revision may merit an explicit field separating availability from operational viability (e.g., self_host_production_grade or self_host_posture). Do not amend now; record as candidate. PRICING METHODOLOGY CALLS: (1) pricing_model='usage' not 'hybrid' (7.11) — pure multi-product metered consumption, no capacity/seat/time dimension in the canonical bill. (2) cloud_entry_price_usd_month=null (7.17) — no fixed-price paid cloud tier; pure pay-as-you-go with $0 floor; first null-cloud-entry record; candidate 7.17 clarification. ENTERPRISE TIER: a PostHog Enterprise plan with a reported ~USD 2,000/month base fee (adds SOC 2 Type II, HIPAA readiness, GDPR, extended retention up to 5-7 years, dedicated support) is reported by 2026 aggregators (userorbit etc.) but NOT yet browser-validated against the live pricing page; enterprise_min_declared_usd left null pending validation (if confirmed at USD 2,000/mo ≈ USD 24,000/yr, it would sit notably close to Grafana's USD 25,000/yr published Enterprise floor — a cross-record observation worth capturing once validated). Note period ambiguity: Grafana's enterprise_min was annual (USD 25,000/yr); PostHog's reported figure is monthly — annualize for comparability when captured. SOURCE VALIDATIONS — most done 2026-05-23 via Claude web_fetch of live pages (Gatsby-static pages + GitHub blobs render fully under web_fetch, so vara-alta achievable from this environment; method documented per-field): (a) DONE — root LICENSE at master HEAD confirmed MIT Expat. (b) DONE — ee/LICENSE at master HEAD confirmed 'PostHog Enterprise License' proprietary. (c) STILL PENDING — Wayback-check root LICENSE across 2020/2022/2024 to confirm core was ALWAYS MIT (no quiet relicense), validating license_history=[] to HIGH; web.archive.org URLs were not in the web_fetch provenance set this session, so this needs Pablo's browser OR a future session where a Wayback URL is seeded. (d) DONE (content) — posthog.com/pricing confirmed: free-tier per-product numbers, '$0/mo' base / no platform/seat fee, Enterprise add-on USD 2,000/mo; STILL TODO: trigger a Wayback SAVE of posthog.com/pricing for forward audit-trail (action, not a read — Pablo or a seeded save URL). (e) DONE — posthog.com/docs/contribute shows full contribution flow with no CLA step → cla_required=false (MEDIUM-HIGH); optional residual: confirm absence of cla-assistant bot on a recent PR. (f) DONE — posthog.com/about confirmed founded 2020 (YC W20, HN launch). HQ (SF) remains MEDIUM (about page is remote-first, no explicit HQ; PrivCo SF address stands). PHASE 7 STRATEGIC SYNTHESIS (Pablo 2026-05-23 — the two framings belong here, not buried in feature_gating): (1) The advanced_analytics='oss' finding is deeper than 'analytics-bi differs from observability/databases'. PostHog exhibits a model where the OPEN OSS value IS the main product, and monetization happens AROUND it — cloud operation, governance, scale convenience, managed infra, compliance/support. Mongo/Grafana/Sentry capture value by RESTRICTING 'advanced' capabilities; PostHog captures value by making the managed operational experience much superior while leaving the analytical core open. This elegantly TENSIONS an implicit Atlas hypothesis ('OSS companies monetize by gating features') with a counter-model: 'you can also monetize by leaving features open but moving the operational friction.' This is the synthesis-layer payload of the record. (2) On multi_region_ha='oss' — correct epistemic discipline: it does NOT strengthen the observability N=2 signal (different category). But it opens a better causal framing than 'category alone': multi_region_ha may depend on DEPLOYMENT TOPOLOGY ECONOMICS — observability → OSS multi-region is cheap because distributed ingest/storage is already in the DNA; analytics-bi cloud-native → multi-region OSS viable because the monetization moat doesn't live there; databases → multi-region/HA directly threatens the core enterprise moat, so it gets gated. NOT a formal hypothesis yet (insufficient N), but a stronger causal frame than category-as-cause. Candidate for the value-capture taxonomy / a future hypothesis report.
